items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
Vague doubts haunt me once again...
Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Identical to the approved reference. It preserves the mock-heroic, theatrical register of «терзают смутные сомнения» and keeps the clipped parallelism «У Шпака — …, у посла — …» without over-explaining. It also correctly avoids turning it into simple possession (“has”), maintaining the comedic implication of the items being implicated/lost rather than merely owned.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: Identical to the approved reference. The translation keeps the repetitive, banal Soviet-style politeness and the absurd self-introduction (“the Tsar”) that creates the era-clash humor, matching the intended comic effect and character voice (Bunsha’s stiff social ritualism).

items.translation: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved reference. It preserves the authoritarian, performative demand and the comic escalation of Bunsha’s bureaucrat-turned-autocrat persona; no loss of force or timing.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Identical to the approved reference. It keeps the elevated, archaic-sounding melancholy and the socially jarring “old woman” address that drives the humor in a modern Soviet domestic setting.

items.translation: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved reference. It correctly makes “demons” the agent and preserves the panicked, superstitious misreading of technology; the repetition and ellipsis keep the comic rhythm.

items.translation: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved reference. It retains the quasi-biblical, proclamatory tone and the religious framing that is key to the joke (faith credited for a mechanical event), without flattening into a casual paraphrase.

items.translation: The floor is open to everyone!
items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved reference. It captures the MC/announcer vibe and the sudden modern-party energy imposed on a historical setting, preserving the comedic “permission to go wild” effect.

items.translation: Now, this was a good call!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved reference. It keeps the breezy self-satisfaction and opportunistic tone of Miloslavsky’s line, conveying the comic understatement of a criminal delighted by “luck.”

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved reference. It correctly avoids calling the eggplant item “caviar” in English (which would miscue the joke), while preserving the punchline structure and the satirical inversion of luxury vs. Soviet everyday food.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: Identical to the approved reference. It preserves the comedic inventory cadence, the self-important “domestic” qualifier, and the trailing, slightly confused counting that signals Shpak’s exaggeration and performative grievance.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Identical to the approved reference. It keeps the calm, sing-song fatalism and the escalating inclusiveness that turns a frightening reality (psychiatric commitment) into shared absurdist comedy.

summary.interpretation: 0.00 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.00 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
